title: The scheme and its gates
paras: Let's start with the scheme and its gates. Schedule 2, Part 1 of the G-P-D-O grants planning permission nationally for works within the curtilage of a dwellinghouse. There are nine classes. Class A covers extensions and alterations. Class A-A, additional storeys. Classes B and C, roof enlargements and other roof alterations. Class D, porches. Class E, outbuildings. Class F, hard surfaces. Class G, chimneys and flues. And Class H, microwave antennae. Most domestic building work in England proceeds under these rights rather than by application — which is exactly why they repay careful reading before anyone orders materials.

Three gates apply before any class is opened. First, Part 1 serves dwellinghouses only. Flats and maisonettes have no householder rights, and nor does a house divided into flats. Second, a dwelling that exists only by virtue of a Part 3 change-of-use right — that's Classes G, M, M-A, N, P, P-A or Q — is expressly excluded from the whole of Part 1. So the barn converted under Class Q, and the office converted under Class M-A, have no householder rights at all. No extensions, no outbuildings, no porch. And third, the use must actually be as a dwellinghouse. The rights attach to the use, not the bricks.

Every principal allowance is measured against the original dwellinghouse — the house as first built, or as it stood on the 1st of July 1948 if it's older than that. Extensions added since then count against the allowance today, whoever built them, with or without permission. A rear addition from the nineteen-seventies can mean a present-day owner has no Class A headroom left — and neither the estate agent nor the deeds will say so.

Article 2(3) land — that's conservation areas, National Parks, the Broads, National Landscapes and World Heritage Sites — carries tighter rules throughout the Part. Under Class A alone, it removes cladding, side extensions and multi-storey rear extensions. And Class B, the dormer class, is excluded on such land entirely. Separately, rights can be removed on any land by an article 4 direction, or by a condition on an existing permission — and modern estate permissions remove them routinely.

So the working sequence is fixed. Establish that the building is a house, and how it came to be one. Establish what the original house looked like. Establish what land it stands on. Establish whether anything has removed the rights. And establish what's already been built. Only then do you open the class and start measuring.

title: Class A: extensions day to day
paras: Now to Class A — extensions, day to day. Class A permits the enlargement, improvement or other alteration of a dwellinghouse, and it carries the arithmetic most people mean by permitted development. A single-storey rear extension may extend four metres beyond the original rear wall of a detached house, or three metres in any other case, to a maximum height of four metres. And where any part of the extension comes within two metres of any boundary of the curtilage, its eaves may not exceed three metres.

An extension of more than one storey must not extend beyond the original rear wall by more than three metres. It must be at least seven metres from the rear boundary, and its roof pitch must match the existing house so far as practicable. Upper floors bring the neighbour-facing conditions into play. Any upper-floor window in a side elevation must be obscure-glazed and non-opening — unless the opening parts are more than one point seven metres above the floor of the room.

Side extensions are permitted only as a single storey, to a maximum height of four metres, and no wider than half the width of the original dwellinghouse. On article 2(3) land there's no side extension right at all. Nor any multi-storey rear extension. Nor exterior cladding in stone, artificial stone, pebble dash, render, timber, plastic or tiles.

The general limits sit over everything. Nothing forward of the principal elevation where it fronts a highway. Nothing higher than the highest part of the existing roof. Eaves no higher than the existing eaves. Total coverage by buildings within the curtilage capped at fifty per cent, excluding the original house. And no verandas, balconies or raised platforms — a platform counts as raised once it exceeds thirty centimetres. Materials must be of similar appearance to the existing house — except for conservatories.

Measurement discipline decides these cases. Heights are taken from the highest part of the adjacent natural ground. Depth runs from the original rear wall — not the current one. And a wraparound extension has to satisfy the side limits and the rear limits at the same time, which in practice it rarely can. Where the built dimensions exceed the limits by any margin, the whole extension is unauthorised. Part 1 has no concept of a minor breach.

title: The larger home extension route
paras: Next, the larger home extension route. Paragraph A one, sub-paragraph G, doubles the single-storey rear allowance — to eight metres for a detached house, and six metres for any other — through the neighbour consultation scheme. It's prior notification with teeth. The right exists only if the process is followed to the letter, and the enlarged allowance isn't available on article 2(3) land, or for anything other than a single-storey rear extension.

The developer submits a written description stating the depth beyond the original rear wall, the maximum height, and the eaves height — together with a site plan and the addresses of adjoining premises. The authority notifies adjoining owners and occupiers, who have twenty-one days to object. If any adjoining neighbour objects, the authority must assess the effect on the amenity of adjoining premises — and only that — and grant or refuse prior approval accordingly.

Work may begin only on one of three events. First, written notice that prior approval is not required. Second, written notice granting prior approval. Or third, the expiry of forty-two days from the authority receiving the notification, without any decision. Silence after forty-two days is therefore a lawful start — which is unusual among prior approval regimes, and widely disbelieved by clients and authorities alike.

The development must be built in accordance with the details submitted, or as approved, and the stated dimensions are the enforceable envelope. The assessment, where it's triggered, is confined to adjoining amenity. Design, character and highway objections have no purchase within this scheme — however firmly they're made.

title: Roofs: Classes B and C
paras: On to the roof — Classes B and C. Class B permits the enlargement of the roof: dormers and hip-to-gable conversions. It works within a cubic content allowance — forty cubic metres for a terrace house, fifty cubic metres for any other, measured externally. And the allowance is cumulative. Any previous enlargement of the roof, by any owner at any time, counts against it. Class B isn't available on article 2(3) land at all.

The enlargement must not exceed the highest part of the existing roof, and must not extend beyond the plane of the existing roof slope on the principal elevation where it fronts a highway. Other than hip-to-gable works, it must be set back at least twenty centimetres from the original eaves, measured along the roof slope. Materials must be of similar appearance. Side-facing windows carry the obscure-glazed, non-opening condition. And no veranda, balcony or raised platform may be formed.

Class C covers roof alterations that don't enlarge — rooflights being the everyday case. The alteration may not project more than fifteen centimetres beyond the plane of the roof slope, and may not rise above the highest part of the roof. Class C survives on article 2(3) land — which is why rooflights remain feasible in conservation areas when dormers are not.

Where the ambition is a whole extra floor rather than a dormer, the choice lies between Class B arithmetic and Class A-A, additional storeys. Different gates, different processes, different politics. The dormer is measured in cubic metres and self-assessed. The storey passes through prior approval, with the authority holding a view on external appearance. Run both calculations before choosing a route.

title: Class AA: additional storeys
paras: Which brings us to Class A-A — additional storeys. Class A-A, inserted in August 2020, permits the construction of up to two additional storeys on a house of two or more storeys, or one additional storey on a single-storey house — subject to gates that exclude much of the stock. The house must have been constructed between the 1st of July 1948 and the 28th of October 2018. It must not exceed eighteen metres in total height once extended. And the right doesn't apply on article 2(3) land, or to houses created by the Part 3 change-of-use rights.

Unlike the rest of Part 1, nothing happens without prior approval. The application is assessed on a long statutory list — including the external appearance of the dwelling, the impact on the amenity of neighbouring premises, and air traffic and defence considerations where relevant. So the authority holds what amounts to a design judgment over the proposal, and refusals on appearance are routine.

The conditions continue after the build. The developer must notify the authority on completion of the development — a duty unique in Part 1, and easily missed. The class also cross-refers to the National Planning Policy Framework by its publication date. S-I 2026 number 313 updated those references to the December 2024 Framework in April 2026 — and they'll fall out of date again whenever a new Framework publishes. So the class must be read against the current amendment position, always.

And there's no government technical guidance on Class A-A at all. The 2019 householder guidance predates it, and has never been revised. The only authoritative sources are the Order itself, and the accumulating appeal decisions.

title: Outbuildings and surfaces: E and F
paras: Now, outbuildings and surfaces — Classes E and F. Class E permits buildings, enclosures, pools and containers required for a purpose incidental to the enjoyment of the dwellinghouse as such. The garden office, the gym, the workshop, the swimming pool. Incidental is the load-bearing word. Primary living accommodation is not incidental — so an outbuilding providing a bedroom, or functioning as a self-contained annexe, is outside the class, however it's labelled on the drawings.

The limits run like this. Single storey only. Maximum height four metres with a dual-pitched roof, three metres otherwise — and two and a half metres for any part within two metres of a curtilage boundary. Eaves capped at two and a half metres throughout. Nothing forward of the principal elevation. And the same fifty per cent curtilage coverage cap shared with extensions — the existing shed and the new garden room count together. Further restrictions apply on article 2(3) land, and in National Parks, the Broads and National Landscapes, where siting and size limits tighten. Check paragraph E one before fixing a position.

Raised platforms above thirty centimetres are excluded here, just as in Class A — which catches decking — and an outbuilding may not contain a microwave antenna. The use limit persists after construction, too. A building erected lawfully as a store becomes unlawful when it becomes a dwelling, and the change is enforceable as a material change of use.

Class F permits hard surfaces incidental to the enjoyment of the dwellinghouse. The single operative condition bites at the front. Where a new or replacement surface between the principal elevation and a highway exceeds five square metres, it must either be made of porous materials, or drain to a permeable area within the curtilage. The rule exists for surface-water flooding — and it's enforced most often after the flood.

title: Porches, chimneys, antennae — and proving it
paras: Finally — porches, chimneys, antennae, and proving it. The small classes finish the domestic set. Class D permits a porch outside any external door: a maximum of three square metres of ground area, measured externally, a maximum height of three metres, and no part within two metres of any boundary with a highway. Class G permits chimneys, flues and soil pipes, provided they don't rise one metre or more above the highest part of the roof — with siting restrictions on designated land. And Class H permits up to two microwave antennae, subject to size limits — broadly one metre, with only one antenna over sixty centimetres — a cubic capacity cap of thirty-five litres, chimney-mounting rules, and visibility restrictions on article 2(3) land. Conditions require siting to minimise visual impact, and removal when the antenna is no longer needed.

Then comes the part every simplified guide omits. Permitted development is self-assessed. No one checks the measurements, no one issues anything, and the owner's confidence is not a legal status. The only instrument that certifies lawfulness is a certificate under the 1990 Act — section 192 for proposed works, section 191 for works done — determined on the balance of probabilities, against evidence.

The certificate discipline matters at sale. Purchasers' solicitors increasingly ask for proof that unconsented works were lawful, and a file of dated drawings, measurements and photographs — or better, a certificate — answers in a way that recollection cannot. Where any dimension sits near a limit, the section 192 certificate before the build is the cheap insurance. The section 191 after a dispute is the expensive kind.

And one last thing — the boundary of the subject. Part 1 answers the planning question only. Building regulations approval, the Party Wall etc. Act, private covenants, drainage easements and protected species law all apply on their own terms — and none of them has read the G-P-D-O. The lawful extension that breaches a covenant is still a breach of covenant.
